executive_brief: The threat landscape has fundamentally shifted in the past week. We are witnessing the collapse of the traditional zero-day timeline, driven by the emergence of agentic AI capable of discovering and weaponizing vulnerabilities at machine speed. Anthropic's 'Mythos' AI model has identified thousands of previously unknown flaws, while attackers are already exploiting AI-native platforms like Flowise and Marimo within hours of disclosure [113][117][248]. This acceleration has compressed the ransomware kill chain; the Storm-1175 group now deploys Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of initial breach, leveraging zero-days to bypass defenses before patches can be applied [3][58]. Simultaneously, state-sponsored actors have moved from espionage to physical disruption. Iran-linked groups are actively compromising Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) in US critical infrastructure, while a separate incident saw hackers claiming control over Venice's flood defense pumps [123][315]. Security teams must assume that any exposed asset is already compromised and that the window for remediation has shrunk from weeks to hours.

threat_stories.headline: The AI Zero-Day Arms Race: Mythos and the Collapse of Patch Windows
threat_stories.narrative:
The era of human-paced vulnerability discovery is over. On April 8, 2026, Anthropic unveiled 'Claude Mythos,' an AI model capable of autonomously identifying thousands of high-severity zero-day vulnerabilities across major operating systems and browsers [114][126]. This capability, part of 'Project Glasswing,' represents a dual-use threat: while intended for defense, the underlying technology lowers the barrier for adversaries to generate exploits at scale. The immediate impact is already visible in the wild. On April 10, a critical Remote Code Execution (RCE) flaw in the Marimo Python notebook (CVE-2026-39987) was exploited within 10 hours of disclosure [294]. Similarly, the Flowise AI agent builder suffered active exploitation of CVE-2025-59528, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary code via the CustomMCP node [57][90].

This trend indicates that AI-driven tools are not just finding bugs; they are enabling attackers to weaponize them faster than defenders can patch. The 'Mythos' model reportedly found a 13-year-old RCE bug in Apache ActiveMQ within minutes, a task that would take human researchers weeks [248]. The implication is clear: the 'patch window' for critical vulnerabilities is collapsing. Organizations relying on traditional monthly patch cycles are now obsolete. The speed of discovery has outpaced the speed of remediation, creating a permanent state of exposure for unpatched systems.

Defenders must pivot from a reactive posture to a 'prevention-first' strategy. This involves implementing architectural mitigations like micro-segmentation and data minimization, rather than relying solely on vendor patches [119]. The presence of AI agents in development environments (like Flowise and Marimo) introduces a new attack surface where prompt injection and tool misuse can lead to full system compromise [120]. Security teams must audit all AI-integrated workflows immediately, assuming that any unauthenticated API endpoint is a potential entry point for automated exploitation.

timeline.event: Anthropic announces Claude Mythos AI model capable of finding zero-day vulnerabilities.
timeline.significance: Signals the beginning of AI-driven vulnerability discovery at scale.

timeline.event: Flowise AI platform suffers active exploitation of CVE-2025-59528.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates immediate weaponization of AI platform vulnerabilities.

timeline.event: Marimo RCE flaw (CVE-2026-39987) exploited within 10 hours of disclosure.
timeline.significance: Confirms the collapse of the patch window to single-digit hours.

timeline.event: Mythos AI finds 13-year-old ActiveMQ RCE bug in minutes.
timeline.significance: Proves AI can outperform human researchers in legacy code analysis.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. All organizations using AI development tools (Flowise, Marimo, LangChain) are at immediate risk. The broader software supply chain faces increased instability as AI accelerates exploit development.
threat_stories.action_required: Audit all AI agent configurations. Disable unauthenticated WebSocket endpoints. Implement network segmentation for AI development environments. Prioritize patching for AI-native platforms over traditional software.

threat_stories.headline: Storm-1175: The 24-Hour Ransomware Kill Chain
threat_stories.narrative:
The Medusa ransomware group, operating under the alias Storm-1175, has fundamentally altered the economics of ransomware attacks. Microsoft reports that this group now transitions from initial access to ransomware deployment within 24 hours [38][58]. This velocity is achieved by chaining zero-day and N-day vulnerabilities, allowing them to bypass security controls before defenders can detect lateral movement. The group has exploited over 16 vulnerabilities since 2023, often weaponizing fresh bugs within days of disclosure [58].

The operational model has shifted from 'breach and hold' to 'breach and burn.' Storm-1175 uses zero-days to gain initial access, then rapidly exfiltrates data and deploys Medusa ransomware before traditional EDR solutions can trigger alerts [3]. This speed renders traditional incident response timelines ineffective. By the time a security team identifies the initial breach, the attackers have already encrypted critical systems and exfiltrated sensitive data. The group specifically targets healthcare and education sectors in the UK, US, and Australia, exploiting the high-value nature of their data [3].

This tactic requires a shift in defense strategy. Organizations must assume that perimeter defenses will fail and focus on detection of anomalous behavior rather than signature-based prevention. Network segmentation is critical to limit the blast radius of a compromised endpoint. Additionally, offline backups must be immutable and tested regularly, as the speed of encryption leaves little time for recovery from live systems.

timeline.event: Microsoft links Storm-1175 to Medusa ransomware zero-day attacks.
timeline.significance: Confirms the use of zero-days for rapid ransomware deployment.

timeline.event: Reports confirm Storm-1175 exploits zero-days within 24 hours of disclosure.
Storm-1175 deploys Medusa against healthcare and education sectors.
timeline.significance: Confirms sector targeting and active exploitation.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Healthcare and education sectors are primary targets. The 24-hour window makes traditional incident response obsolete.
threat_stories.action_required: Implement network segmentation to isolate critical assets. Enforce immutable offline backups. Monitor for rapid lateral movement and data exfiltration patterns.

threat_stories.headline: Iranian Actors Target US Critical Infrastructure PLCs
threat_stories.narrative:
Iran-affiliated threat actors have escalated their campaign against US critical infrastructure, moving from espionage to active disruption of Operational Technology (OT). Since November 2023, these actors have compromised at least 75 Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) across energy and water sectors [123]. The attackers exploit internet-exposed Rockwell Automation and Siemens PLCs, often left vulnerable due to insecure configurations and legacy protocols like Modbus and EtherNet/IP [135]. On April 8, 2026, CISA and the FBI issued a joint advisory warning that these actors are actively manipulating project files and altering SCADA/HMI display data to cause operational disruption [129].

The campaign is not limited to the US. On April 12, 2026, a separate incident saw hackers claiming control over the Venice San Marco anti-flood pumps, demonstrating the physical risks of exposed OT systems [315]. The Iranian actors utilize leased overseas infrastructure to mask their origin and deploy SSH tools like Dropbear for persistent remote access [123]. The goal is clear: to degrade the reliability of critical services and create economic instability. The exposure of 5,219 Rockwell Automation PLCs globally, primarily in the US, highlights the scale of the vulnerability [306].

This threat requires immediate action from OT security teams. Internet-facing PLCs must be disconnected or protected by firewalls blocking specific OT ports (44818, 502). Organizations must audit their OT environments for default credentials and unnecessary services. The convergence of IT and OT networks has created a pathway for attackers to move from corporate networks to physical control systems, making segmentation between IT and OT environments a critical control.

timeline.event: FBI/CISA warn of Iran-linked actors targeting US critical infrastructure PLCs.
timeline.significance: Official government warning of active OT targeting.

timeline.event: CISA identifies 5,219 exposed Rockwell Automation PLCs globally.
timeline.significance: Quantifies the scale of the vulnerability.

timeline.event: Hackers claim control over Venice flood defense pumps.
timeline.significance: Demonstrates physical impact of OT compromise.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Direct threat to physical safety and operational continuity in energy, water, and manufacturing sectors.
threat_stories.action_required: Disconnect PLCs from the internet. Block OT-specific ports at the firewall. Audit for default credentials. Implement network segmentation between IT and OT.

threat_stories.headline: APT28 Hijacks Home Routers for Global DNS Espionage
threat_stories.narrative:
The Russian state-sponsored group APT28 (Forest Blizzard) has launched a sophisticated campaign targeting home and small-office routers to conduct Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) attacks. Since May 2025, the group has compromised insecure MikroTik and TP-Link routers to hijack DNS settings [81]. By redirecting DNS traffic, attackers can intercept TLS connections and steal Microsoft 365 credentials, specifically targeting Outlook sessions [139]. The campaign utilizes utilities like dnsmasq to spoof DNS responses and present invalid TLS certificates to intercept plaintext traffic [139].

The US government disrupted this operation on April 8, 2026, but the technique remains a significant threat [147]. The campaign highlights the vulnerability of the 'home office' perimeter. As remote work persists, the security of residential internet infrastructure has become a critical component of enterprise defense. APT28 leverages the trust users place in their home network equipment to bypass corporate security controls. The group has also deployed PRISMEX malware in related campaigns targeting Ukraine and NATO allies, using RTF file exploits to force WebDAV connections [167].

Defenders must treat home routers as critical security assets. Organizations should enforce DNS over HTTPS (DoH) on client devices to prevent DNS hijacking. Hardware tokens for authentication should be mandatory for privileged accounts, as credential theft via AiTM is a primary goal. Employees should be educated on the risks of default router credentials and the importance of firmware updates.

timeline.event: APT28 exploits SOHO routers for global DNS hijacking campaign.
timeline.significance: Confirms the targeting of residential infrastructure.

timeline.event: US authorities disrupt the router hacking campaign.
timeline.significance: Confirms active government response to the threat.

timeline.event: APT28 deploys PRISMEX malware targeting Ukraine and NATO.
timeline.significance: Links router campaign to broader espionage efforts.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: High. Risk of credential theft and session hijacking for remote workers. Compromises the integrity of enterprise authentication.
threat_stories.action_required: Enforce DNS over HTTPS. Require hardware tokens for MFA. Audit home router configurations for remote management settings.

threat_stories.headline: The Vulnerability Tsunami: Fortinet, Adobe, and Ivanti
threat_stories.narrative:
The week saw a surge in critical vulnerabilities being actively exploited in the wild. On April 6, Fortinet released an emergency hotfix for CVE-2026-35616, a high-severity flaw in FortiClient EMS allowing unauthenticated remote code execution [6][102]. CISA added this to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities (KEV) catalog, mandating patching by federal agencies [29]. Simultaneously, Adobe Reader was found to be under active exploitation via a zero-day vulnerability (CVE-2026-34621) that had been in use for months [223][305]. Attackers used malicious PDFs to fingerprint targets and exfiltrate data without user interaction [239].

Ivanti also faced a critical exploit with CVE-2026-1340 in Endpoint Manager Mobile, allowing unauthenticated remote code execution [170]. CISA ordered federal agencies to patch this flaw by Sunday, April 12 [162]. These vulnerabilities share a common trait: they allow attackers to bypass authentication and execute code on critical management systems. The speed of exploitation suggests that automated scanners are actively probing for these specific CVEs.

Organizations must prioritize patching for these specific vulnerabilities immediately. For Fortinet, apply the emergency hotfix to EMS 7.4.5 and 7.4.6. For Adobe, disable JavaScript in Reader settings until a patch is applied. For Ivanti, apply the latest patch and run the exploitation detection RPM. The convergence of these exploits indicates a coordinated effort to compromise management and endpoint security tools.

timeline.event: Fortinet releases emergency hotfix for CVE-2026-35616.
timeline.significance: Confirms active exploitation of FortiClient EMS.

timeline.event: CISA orders feds to patch Ivanti EPMM flaw (CVE-2026-1340).
timeline.significance: Highlights the critical nature of the vulnerability.

timeline.event: Adobe Reader zero-day (CVE-2026-34621) confirmed active for months.
Adobe patches the actively exploited Reader flaw.

threat_stories.impact_assessment: Critical. Immediate risk of system compromise for organizations using Fortinet EMS, Adobe Reader, and Ivanti EPMM.
threat_stories.action_required: Patch Fortinet EMS to 7.4.7. Disable Adobe Reader JavaScript. Patch Ivanti EPMM immediately. Monitor for exploitation indicators.

actor_profiles.name: Storm-1175
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Deploying Medusa ransomware within 24 hours of initial breach using zero-day vulnerabilities. Targeting healthcare and education sectors.

actor_profiles.name: APT28 (Forest Blizzard)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Hijacking home and SOHO routers to conduct DNS hijacking and AiTM attacks against Microsoft 365. Deploying PRISMEX malware.

actor_profiles.name: Iran-Linked APT (CyberAv3ngers)
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Targeting internet-exposed PLCs in US critical infrastructure. Manipulating SCADA/HMI data to cause operational disruption.

actor_profiles.name: Lazarus Group
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Stealing $285M from Drift protocol. Spreading malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go, and Rust. Targeting Node.js maintainers.

actor_profiles.name: Anthropic Mythos
actor_profiles.recent_activity: Identifying thousands of zero-day vulnerabilities across major software systems. Capable of finding legacy bugs in minutes.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Improper access control vulnerability allowing unauthenticated attackers to execute unauthorized code via API.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Install emergency hotfix for EMS 7.4.5 and 7.4.6. Upgrade to 7.4.7.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Pre-authenticated remote code execution via WebSocket endpoint.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Upgrade Marimo to version 0.23.0 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Code injection vulnerability in CustomMCP node allowing remote code execution.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Flowise to version 3.0.6 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Zero-day vulnerability allowing arbitrary code execution via malicious PDFs.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update to latest patched version. Disable JavaScript.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Critical code injection vulnerability allowing unauthenticated remote code execution.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Apply latest patch released by Ivanti.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: Authorization bypass allowing attackers to gain root-level access to host systems.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Update Docker Engine to version 29.3.1 or later.

critical_vulnerabilities.description: RTF file exploitation bypassing security controls to force WebDAV connection.
LNK file exploitation bypassing browser protections to execute code silently.
critical_vulnerabilities.remediation: Install Microsoft patch released on February 10, 2026.

hunting_leads.title: Fortinet EMS API Exploitation
hunting_leads.context: Attackers are actively exploiting CVE-2026-35616 to gain unauthorized access to FortiClient EMS.
hunting_leads.query: index=fortinet sourcetype=fortinet:ems | search "POST /api" AND "unauthorized" | stats count by src_ip

hunting_leads.title: Marimo WebSocket RCE
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-39987 allows unauthenticated RCE via WebSocket endpoint.
hunting_leads.query: index=network sourcetype=web | search "ws://" AND "/terminal/ws" | stats count by src_ip

hunting_leads.title: Flowise CustomMCP Exploitation
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2025-59528 allows arbitrary code execution via CustomMCP node.
hunting_leads.query: index=flowise sourcetype=flowise | search "CustomMCP" AND "Function()" | stats count by user

hunting_leads.title: APT28 DNS Hijacking
hunting_leads.context: APT28 is hijacking DNS to intercept Microsoft 365 traffic.
hunting_leads.query: index=dns sourcetype=dns | search "outlook.office365.com" AND "A" AND "src_ip" IN (home_network) | stats count by dst_ip
indicators: DNS responses to outlook.office365.com from non-Microsoft IPs

hunting_leads.title: Adobe Reader Zero-Day
hunting_leads.context: CVE-2026-34621 is being exploited via malicious PDFs.
hunting_leads.query: index=endpoint sourcetype=process | search "AcroRd32.exe" AND "JavaScript" | stats count by hostname

statistics.key_changes: The zero-day timeline has collapsed from weeks to hours due to AI-driven discovery. Ransomware kill chains are now under 24 hours.
declining_threats: Traditional phishing campaigns (replaced by AI-generated lures)
Long-term espionage campaigns (replaced by rapid ransomware)
